items.correct: Dissolved CO2 forms carbonic acid, lowering ocean pH.
items.misconception: Acid rain falling on the ocean.
items.why: Students think acidification is direct pollution. The main driver is atmospheric CO2 dissolving in seawater - an inevitable result of higher atmospheric concentrations, threatening shell-forming organisms.

items.correct: The maximum population an environment can sustainably support given available resources.
items.misconception: How much an animal can physically carry.
items.random_wrong: The size a species can reach.
items.why: Students take 'carrying' literally. It's an ECOLOGICAL concept - the ceiling set by food, water, space, etc. Populations may temporarily exceed it but then crash, or oscillate around it.

items.q: What is the difference between species richness and species evenness?
items.correct: Richness counts how many species exist; evenness measures how balanced their abundances are.
items.random_wrong: Richness is for plants, evenness for animals.
items.why: Students conflate these. A community with 10 species each having 100 individuals has high evenness; one with 10 species but 991 of species A and 1 of each other has low evenness despite identical richness.

items.q: Norman Borlaug, father of the Green Revolution, won the Nobel Peace Prize in:
items.why: Correct answer: 1970. “1962” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Soil layer containing most organic matter:
items.why: It's O horizon. “B horizon” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Year Borlaug received Nobel Peace Prize?
items.why: The real figure is 1970 AD. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
